corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: Our model for ImageNet with dropout is a CNN which is trained on 224×224 patches randomly extracted from the 256 × 256 images, as well as their horizontal reflections.
In order to achieve state-of-the-art performance on the validation set, we found it necessary to use the very complicated network architecture described above.
Fortunately, the complexity of this architecture is not the main point of our paper.
The TIMIT Acoustic-Phonetic Continuous Speech Corpus is a standard dataset used for evaluation of automatic speech recognition systems.
The TIMIT Acoustic-Phonetic Continuous Speech Corpus is a standard dataset used for evaluation of automatic speech recognition systems.
We used Kaldi, an open source code library for speech , to pre-process the dataset so that our results can be reproduced exactly.
Reuters Corpus Volume I (RCV1-v2) ( ) is an archive of 804,414 newswire stories that have been manually categorized into 103 topics .
We took the dataset and split it into 63 classes based on the the 63 categories at the secondlevel of the category tree.
Each document was represented using the 2000 most frequent non-stopwords in the dataset.
The corpus covers four major groups: corporate/industrial, economics, government/social, and markets.
When a large feedforward neural network is trained on a small training set, it typically performs poorly on held-out test data.
Random "dropout" gives big improvements on many benchmark tasks and sets new records for speech and object recognition.
On each presentation of each training case, each hidden unit is randomly omitted from the network with a probability of 0.5, so a hidden unit cannot rely on other hidden units being present.
On each presentation of each training case, each hidden unit is randomly omitted from the network with a probability of 0.5, so a hidden unit cannot rely on other hidden units being present.
There is almost certainly a different network for each presentation of each training case but all of these networks share the same weights for the hidden units that are present.
We initially explored the effectiveness of dropout using MNIST, a widely used benchmark for machine learning algorithms.
We then applied dropout to TIMIT, a widely used benchmark for recognition of clean speech with a small vocabulary.

Recently, deep, pre-trained, feedforward neural networks that map a short sequence of frames into a probability distribution over HMM states have been shown to outperform tradional Gaussian mixture models on both TIMIT (6) and a variety of more realistic large vocabulary tasks .

Figure shows the frame classification error rate on the core test set of the TIMIT benchmark when the central frame of a window is classified as belonging to the HMM state that is given the highest probability by the neural net.
The neural net has 4 fully-connected hidden layers of 4000 units per layer and 185 "softmax" output units that are subsequently merged into the 39 distinct classes used for the benchmark.
Dropout of 50% of the hidden units significantly improves classification for a variety of different network architectures (see figure ).

To get the frame recognition rate, the class probabilities that the neural network outputs for each frame are given to a decoder which knows about transition probabilities between HMM states and runs the Viterbi algorithm to infer the single best sequence of HMM states.
ImageNet is an extremely challenging object recognition dataset consisting of thousands of high-resolution images of thousands of classes of object .
In order to achieve state-of-the-art performance on the validation set, we found it necessary to use the very complicated network architecture described above.
We show two nets -one with a 2000-2000-1000-50 and another with a 2000-1000-1000-50 architecture trained with and without dropout.

downstream_contract.intended_use: Feed this JSON to a later local open-source model to write an overview.
downstream_contract.grounding_rule: The downstream generator should write from evidence_units.location.sentence and context_window, not from unsupported outside knowledge.
downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
